topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop air hung thick and lukewarm, mirroring the knot tightening in my chest. I stared at the empty chair across the small table, the one where Liam used to always perch, sketching in a worn notebook. Every time I dared to look up, my gaze snagged on him—laughing easily with a new acquaintance, his arm draped casually around her shoulder. A sudden, sharp clench in my jaw made me want to push the chair back, to shatter the fragile peace. I tried to speak, to ask a simple question about his summer plans, but the words caught, dissolving into a dry rasp in my throat. He glanced over, a flicker of polite recognition, nothing more, and then he turned back to his conversation, leaving me stranded in the silence of my own unacknowledged history. I gathered my things with a jerky, almost violent motion, the scrape of my bag against the wooden floor sounding deafeningly loud in the quiet space.

[story 2]

I traced the faded outline of a shared constellation on the ceiling of my cramped bedroom. The silence in the apartment was oppressive, amplifying the frantic drumming inside my ribs. I picked up the old photograph—us, grinning like fools under a summer sky—and held it until the edges felt hot against my palm. Why did he look through it like I was a stranger? I tried calling, my fingers hovering over the screen, the impulse to scream at the phone feeling overwhelming, but I stopped myself. Instead, I paced the worn rug, the rhythm becoming uneven, sharp. Each step was a desperate search for a crack in the facade, a sign that the memory I held was real, that the bond we forged was substantial. When I finally slumped onto the sofa, the weight of the unanswered questions pressed down, making it hard to draw a full breath.

[story 3]

The late afternoon sun slanted through the dusty windowpanes, illuminating the motes dancing in the air, each one a tiny, mocking reminder of what was lost. I found him by the old park bench, and the sight of him, so effortlessly connected to the world, sent a sickening lurch through my stomach. I approached, my footsteps hesitant, almost clumsy. "Hey," I managed, the sound thin and reedy. He turned, his expression shifting from casual ease to mild confusion, a blankness settling over his features. He offered a small, polite smile, the kind one gives a distant acquaintance. The air seemed to evacuate my lungs. I felt a sudden, cold numbness spreading from my fingertips up my arms. I couldn't articulate the wreckage inside, so I just stood there, watching him, my eyes burning with unshed tears, wishing the ground would simply swallow me whole.
